industries.name: High-Intensity Lighting
uses: Xenon short-arc lamps: xenon plasma arc between tungsten electrodes produces an intense, continuous-spectrum white light closely matching sunlight — used in IMAX projectors, digital cinema projectors, solar simulators, searchlights, and stadium lighting

Xenon flash lamps for photographic strobes, laser pumping (Nd:YAG flashlamp-pumped lasers), and high-speed photography — xenon's ability to produce extremely brief, intense flashes makes it irreplaceable for these applications

Automotive HID (high-intensity discharge) xenon headlights: xenon gas arc with metal halide additives produces 3× more light than halogen with better color rendering — largely being replaced by LED headlights in new vehicles
Xenon excimer lasers (XeF at 351nm, XeCl at 308nm) for dermatological treatment of psoriasis, vitiligo, and atopic dermatitis — narrow-band UVB therapy delivered precisely to affected skin areas

companies.name: Ushio America
companies.use: Major xenon short-arc lamp manufacturer. Produces xenon lamps for digital cinema projectors (IMAX, Christie, Barco), solar simulators, and UV curing systems. Major supplier to the professional projection and scientific lighting markets.

companies.name: Osram (ams-OSRAM)
companies.share: ~20% of xenon automotive and specialty lamp market
companies.use: Major xenon HID automotive headlight and specialty lamp producer. Historically supplied xenon headlight burners to premium automotive OEMs (BMW, Mercedes, Audi) and xenon cinema projection lamps to Barco and Christie.

industries.name: Medical Imaging & Anesthesia
uses: Hyperpolarized xenon-129 (¹²⁹Xe) MRI: laser-polarized ¹²⁹Xe gas inhaled by patients produces high-SNR MRI images of lung ventilation and airways — far superior to conventional MRI for detecting emphysema, fibrotic lung disease, and asthma airspace distribution

Xenon as general anesthetic: xenon (60–70% with oxygen) produces rapid, smooth anesthesia with minimal cardiovascular depression and rapid recovery — approved as anesthetic in Germany and Russia. Superior properties but 50× more expensive than conventional volatile anesthetics
Xenon CT ventilation imaging: inhaled xenon gas is detectable by CT scanner, providing regional lung ventilation maps for pulmonary function assessment without radioactive tracers
Xenon neuroprotection: xenon at sub-anesthetic concentrations shows neuroprotective effects in animal models of stroke and neonatal hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy — clinical trials ongoing

companies.name: Air Products
companies.use: Major xenon producer from cryogenic air separation. Supplies medical-grade and research-grade xenon for hyperpolarized MRI, anesthesia applications, and scientific research globally.

companies.name: Polarean Imaging
companies.use: Commercializing hyperpolarized ¹²⁹Xe MRI lung imaging technology. Produces the XENOVIEW polarizer system that spin-polarizes xenon-129 for clinical lung MRI at hospital radiology departments. FDA-approved ¹²⁹Xe MRI contrast agent.

industries.name: Spacecraft Ion Propulsion
uses: Xenon is the propellant of choice for ion thrusters and Hall-effect thrusters on commercial satellites and deep space probes — xenon's high atomic mass maximizes momentum transfer per unit energy, its inertness prevents corrosion, and it liquefies at moderate pressure for compact storage

Hall-effect thrusters using xenon propellant provide station-keeping for geostationary communication satellites (Boeing 702SP, Airbus Eurostar Neo) — replacing liquid bipropellant systems with far lower propellant mass
Deep space missions: NASA Dawn (asteroid belt), Hayabusa (asteroid sample return), BepiColombo (Mercury), and DART (asteroid deflection) all used xenon ion propulsion for primary thrust
SmallSat and CubeSat propulsion: miniaturized xenon electrospray and Hall thrusters for small satellite constellation orbit maintenance and disposal maneuvering

companies.name: Maxar Technologies
companies.use: Major commercial satellite manufacturer using xenon Hall-effect thrusters on its 1300 and 500 series satellite platforms for all-electric orbit raising and station-keeping. Major xenon consumer for satellite propellant.

companies.name: Aerojet Rocketdyne (L3Harris)
companies.use: Major ion and Hall-effect thruster manufacturer. Produced the NEXT-C xenon ion thruster for NASA's Lucy and Psyche missions and the XR-5 Hall thruster for commercial satellite applications.

industries.name: Semiconductor Manufacturing
uses: Xenon difluoride (XeF₂) as isotropic silicon etchant for MEMS fabrication — XeF₂ spontaneously etches silicon at room temperature without plasma, producing smooth, isotropic profiles for microactuator release and sensor fabrication
Xenon plasma in physical vapor deposition (PVD) sputtering: heavy xenon ions provide more efficient sputtering of refractory targets (molybdenum, tungsten) than argon in specialized processes

Xenon flash anneal for ultra-shallow junction formation in advanced CMOS — millisecond xenon flash heating activates dopants without significant diffusion, enabling sub-10nm source/drain junction profiles

companies.name: SPTS Technologies (Orbotech / KLA)
companies.use: Major MEMS process equipment supplier. Its Xactix XeF₂ etch systems use xenon difluoride for isotropic silicon release etching of MEMS inertial sensors, microphones, and RF switches — a standard process step in MEMS device fabrication.
